     1	//! Typed stream-stop classification for the Stream Restart Protocol (SRP).
     2	//!
     3	//! The drain loop reports *why* a provider stream stopped so the restart
     4	//! engine ([`super::super::restart`]) can decide whether re-opening a fresh
     5	//! stream is sound. LLM streams are not resumable mid-flight, so restarts are
     6	//! only sound for [`StreamStop::ColdStall`], [`StreamStop::PrematureEnd`], and
     7	//! transient [`StreamStop::Fault`]: each means a clean re-request yields one
     8	//! complete answer (any partial is discarded, never token-stitched).
     9	
    10	use crate::provider::CompletionResponse;
    11	
    12	/// Reason a provider stream stopped.
    13	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
    14	pub(crate) enum StreamStop {
    15	    /// Provider signalled normal end: a `Done` chunk was observed.
    16	    Clean,
    17	    /// Idle timeout before any chunk arrived — nothing committed; retryable.
    18	    ColdStall,
    19	    /// Idle timeout after partial content — partial returned, not retried.
    20	    MidStreamStall,
    21	    /// Byte stream ended (`None`) *before* any `Done` chunk. The HTTP body
    22	    /// closed mid-turn; the model never signalled completion. Restart-eligible
    23	    /// even if partial content was committed, because re-requesting a fresh
    24	    /// stream yields one complete answer (LLM streams are not token-resumable,
    25	    /// so the partial is discarded rather than stitched).
    26	    PrematureEnd,
    27	    /// Terminal error chunk; retryable only if `transient`. Carries the
    28	    /// provider's error message so the final failure surfaces the cause
    29	    /// (e.g. an OpenAI 400 "model is not supported" body) instead of an
    30	    /// opaque "stream faulted" line.
    31	    Fault { transient: bool, message: String },

    36	    pub(crate) fn restart_eligible(&self) -> bool {
    37	        matches!(
    38	            self,
    39	            StreamStop::ColdStall
    40	                | StreamStop::PrematureEnd
    41	                | StreamStop::Fault {
    42	                    transient: true,
    43	                    ..

    51	    /// [`StreamStop::MidStreamStall`] keeps its partial instead.

   1 | //! Convert prompt task results into runtime notices.
   2 | //!
   3 | //! Prompt execution is usually run as an asynchronous task, so there are two
   4 | //! layers of outcome to interpret: whether the task itself panicked, and whether
   5 | //! the prompt run returned an application-level error. This module normalizes
   6 | //! both outcomes into [`SessionNotice`] values while preserving the associated
   7 | //! [`Session`] for the runtime or UI code that receives the notice.
   8 | 
   9 | use crate::session::Session;
  10 | 
  11 | use super::SessionNotice;
  12 | 
  13 | /// Result returned by a prompt execution task.
  14 | ///
  15 | /// The outer [`Result`] reports whether the task completed normally or panicked.
  16 | /// The inner [`anyhow::Result`] reports whether the prompt run itself succeeded
  17 | /// or returned an error after the task completed normally.
  18 | ///
  19 | /// The three meaningful states are:
  20 | ///
  21 | /// - `Ok(Ok(()))`: the prompt run finished successfully.
  22 | /// - `Ok(Err(_))`: the prompt run completed but returned an error.
  23 | /// - `Err(_)`: the task panicked and yielded a panic payload.
  24 | pub(super) type PromptRunResult = Result<anyhow::Result<()>, Box<dyn std::any::Any + Send>>;
  25 | 
  26 | /// Convert a completed prompt task result into a session notice.
  27 | ///
  28 | /// Successful prompt runs become [`SessionNotice::Finished`]. Returned
  29 | /// application errors and task panics become [`SessionNotice::Failed`], with the
  30 | /// error message converted into displayable text.
  31 | ///
  32 | /// # Arguments
  33 | ///
  34 | /// * `result` - The prompt task outcome to classify.
  35 | /// * `session` - The session that was being processed by the prompt task. It is
  36 | ///   moved into the returned notice.
  37 | ///
  38 | /// # Returns
  39 | ///
  40 | /// A [`SessionNotice`] describing whether the session finished successfully or
  41 | /// failed.
  42 | ///
  43 | /// # Side Effects
  44 | ///
  45 | /// This function has no side effects. It does not modify external state, write
  46 | /// logs, or persist the session.
  47 | pub(super) fn notice(result: PromptRunResult, session: Session) -> SessionNotice {
  48 |     match result {
  49 |         Ok(Ok(())) => SessionNotice::Finished(session),
  50 |         Ok(Err(error)) => SessionNotice::Failed {
  51 |             session,
  52 |             error: error.to_string(),
  53 |         },
  54 |         Err(error) => SessionNotice::Failed {
  55 |             session,
  56 |             error: format!("Provider task panicked: {}", panic_message(&error)),
  57 |         },
  58 |     }
  59 | }
  60 | 
  61 | /// Return a human-readable message from a panic payload.
  62 | ///
  63 | /// Tokio and other task runtimes expose panic payloads as boxed [`std::any::Any`]
  64 | /// values. Most Rust panics carry either a [`String`] or a string slice, and
  65 | /// this helper extracts those common forms without taking ownership of the
  66 | /// payload.
  67 | ///
  68 | /// # Arguments
  69 | ///
  70 | /// * `error` - Panic payload captured from a failed prompt task.
  71 | ///
  72 | /// # Returns

  74 | /// The panic message when the payload is a `String` or `&str`; otherwise,

  81 | fn panic_message(error: &Box<dyn std::any::Any + Send>) -> &str {

  19 | /// Shutdown requests also notify the executor and ask the outer loop to stop.

  36 | /// `false`.

  41 | /// cancellation notifier depending on the command.
  42 | pub(super) async fn handle(
